description: Replace the placeholder README with the first credible Awesome AI Agent Factories foundation document: identity header, scope, Contents navigation, ten category skeletons, and footer contribution/license notes—without bulk content seeding.

context.customerAsk: Phases 0 and 1 foundation: replace the placeholder README with the first strong `Awesome AI Agent Factories` document. Add the Awesome badge, a lightweight header/logo treatment, short neutral description, explicit scope, contents, and the required section skeletons: Theories, Coordination Patterns, Frameworks, Protocols and Interfaces, Benchmarks, Research Papers, Blog Posts, Case Studies, Examples and Templates, and Related Lists. The scope must include multi-agent orchestration, teams/swarms/crews/societies/supervisors, task delegation/routing/handoffs/flow control, agent communication protocols, shared state/workspaces, group-level benchmarks/evaluation, research on coordination/cooperation/competition/organization, and production case studies. The scope must exclude generic single-agent chatbots, generic LLM SDKs, prompt collections, standalone RAG tools, generic observability tools, generic workflow engines with no agent-specific usage, and model provider SDKs unless directly relevant to agent groups or flows. Add contribution and license notes at the bottom without adding a large content inventory yet. Acceptance: README presents a credible curated awesome list, all anchors in Contents match headings, wording is factual and non-promotional, and the file no longer reads as a generic AI agents list.
context.problem: The repository currently has a one-line placeholder README that provides no scope, taxonomy, or trust signal. Without a focused Awesome-list foundation, later governance, review policy, automation, and content seeding lack a coherent public contract, and the project is easily mistaken for a generic AI agents roundup.
context.solution: Ship a single structured README.md that establishes Awesome list identity, defines agent-factory scope with explicit include/exclude rules, provides working Contents navigation to ten category skeletons, and closes with lightweight contribution and license notes—deferring bulk link inventory to later phases.

acceptanceCriteria: README.md replaces the placeholder and presents a credible Awesome list focused on managing groups of AI agents and their flows.
The document includes the Awesome badge, lightweight header/logo treatment, neutral description, explicit scope, Contents section, and all ten required category skeletons.

Scope includes multi-agent orchestration, teams/swarms/crews/societies/supervisors, delegation/routing/handoffs/flow control, communication protocols, shared state/workspaces, group-level evaluation, coordination research, and production case studies; scope excludes generic single-agent chatbots, LLM SDKs, prompt collections, standalone RAG, generic observability, non-agent workflow engines, and model provider SDKs unless directly relevant to agent groups or flows.
Every Contents anchor resolves to the matching section heading when rendered on GitHub.
Wording is factual and non-promotional; the README does not read as a generic AI agents list.
Contribution and license notes appear at the bottom without a large content inventory.
Quality gate: typecheck, lint, and tests pass for any repository tooling invoked by the change (or pass vacuously when no such tooling exists yet).

userStories.title: Establish README identity and opening narrative
userStories.description: As a visitor landing on the repository, I want a clear title, Awesome badge, and concise explanation of agent factories so I know what this list is about before scrolling.
acceptanceCriteria: README.md opens with `# Awesome AI Agent Factories` and the standard Awesome badge linking to https://awesome.re
A lightweight header or logo treatment appears near the top (banner image, SVG, or restrained typographic mark)
A short neutral blockquote tagline describes curated theories, frameworks, benchmarks, research, and writing about managing groups of AI agents and their flows
One or two factual sentences define agent factories as systems for coordinating groups of agents (roles, routing, handoffs, supervision, shared state, group-level evaluation)
The placeholder heading `# awesome-agent-factories` is fully removed
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Define inclusive and exclusive scope
userStories.description: As a contributor or maintainer, I want an explicit Scope section so I can tell whether a resource fits before opening a pull request.
acceptanceCriteria: A `## Scope` section states the list focuses on AI agent factories: systems, theories, frameworks, and practices for managing groups of agents and their flows

An Included list covers multi-agent orchestration; agent teams, swarms, crews, societies, and supervisors; task delegation, routing, handoffs, and flow control; agent communication protocols; shared state and shared workspaces; group-level benchmarks and evaluation methods; research on agent coordination, cooperation, competition, and organization; and production case studies involving groups of agents

An Excluded unless directly relevant list covers single-agent chatbots; generic LLM SDKs; prompt collections; standalone RAG tools; generic observability tools; generic workflow engines with no agent-specific usage; and model provider SDKs
Scope language is neutral and factual with no superlatives or vendor promotion
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Add Contents navigation aligned to categories
userStories.description: As a reader, I want a table of contents so I can jump directly to the category I care about.
acceptanceCriteria: A `## Contents` section lists exactly these entries in order: Theories; Coordination Patterns; Frameworks; Protocols and Interfaces; Benchmarks; Research Papers; Blog Posts; Case Studies; Examples and Templates; Related Lists
Each Contents entry is a markdown link whose anchor target matches the GitHub-rendered slug for the corresponding `##` section heading
Contents appears after the opening narrative and scope, before the first category section
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Add required category section skeletons
userStories.description: As a future contributor, I want every category to exist as a labeled section so I know where entries will live when content seeding begins.
acceptanceCriteria: README.md contains `##` headings for all ten categories: Theories; Coordination Patterns; Frameworks; Protocols and Interfaces; Benchmarks; Research Papers; Blog Posts; Case Studies; Examples and Templates; Related Lists
Each section includes at most one short neutral sentence describing what belongs there (no bullet inventories of specific projects, papers, or URLs)
Sections contain no fabricated entries; they may be empty aside from the descriptive sentence
Section order matches the Contents list order
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Add contribution and license footer notes
userStories.description: As a prospective contributor, I want bottom-of-page guidance on how to contribute and how list content is licensed.
acceptanceCriteria: A contribution note near the end explains that contributions are welcome and points readers to CONTRIBUTING.md for submission rules
A license note states that list content is intended to be released under CC0-1.0 and that linked third-party resources retain their own licenses
Footer notes are concise and do not duplicate a large entry inventory
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Converge README quality and anchor correctness
userStories.description: As a maintainer reviewing the foundation batch, I want confidence that the README reads as a focused agent-factories list and that navigation works end-to-end.
acceptanceCriteria: Clicking every Contents link in a GitHub-rendered preview jumps to the correct section heading
A reader skimming the full document can distinguish this list from a generic AI agents roundup because scope, intro, and category descriptions emphasize groups, flows, orchestration, and coordination
No promotional phrases such as revolutionary, game-changing, or best-in-class appear outside official resource titles
README.md is the only user-facing list document changed for this work item; no unrelated repository cleanup is bundled in
Typecheck passes
